title: A Fixed-Point Approach to Stable Matchings and Some Applications
abstract: We describe a fixed-point based approach to the theory of bipartite stable matchings. By this, we provide a common framework that links together seemingly distant results, like the stable marriage theorem of Gale and Shapley, the Mendelsohn-Dulmage theorem, the Kundu-Lawler theorem, Tarski's fixed-point theorem, the Cantor-Bernstein theorem, Pym's linking theorem, or the monochromatic path theorem of Sands et al. In this framework, we formulate a matroid-generalization of the stable marriage theorem and study the lattice structure of generalized stable matchings. Based on the theory of lattice polyhedra and blocking polyhedra, we extend results of Vande Vate and Rothblum on the bipartite stable matching polytope.
summary_en: Many seemingly distant theorems about stable matchings, fixed points, and lattice structure turn out to be facets of the same underlying mathematical object. Fleiner provides a unified fixed-point-based framework that links together the stable-marriage theorem of Gale and Shapley, the Mendelsohn-Dulmage theorem, the Kundu-Lawler theorem, Tarski's fixed-point theorem, the Cantor-Bernstein theorem, Pym's linking theorem, and the monochromatic-path theorem. Within this framework he formulates a matroid generalisation of the stable marriage theorem and studies the lattice structure of generalised stable matchings. Building on lattice and blocking polyhedra theory, he extends Vande Vate and Rothblum's results on the bipartite stable-matching polytope.
data_used: Theoretical paper; no empirical data. Mathematical proofs and constructions in the language of fixed-point theory, lattice theory, and matroid theory.
policy_relevance: Foundational mathematical paper underlying the matching-markets cluster's applied work. Indirectly relevant for any Hungarian application of stable-matching mechanisms (kidney exchange, school choice, residency matching) — they all rest on the theoretical scaffolding this paper helps build. Useful methodological reference for Hungarian operations-research and mechanism-design researchers.
